## GRAPH TREE NEURAL NETWORKS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In the field of deep learning, various architectures have been developed. However, most studies are limited to specific tasks or datasets due to their fixed layer structure. In this paper, we do not express the structure delivering information as a network model but as a data structure called a graph tree. And we propose two association models of graph tree neural networks(GTNNs) designed to solve the problems of existing networks by analyzing the structure of human neural networks. Defining the starting and ending points in a single graph is difficult, and a tree cannot express the relationship among sibling nodes. On the contrary, a graph tree(GT) can express leaf and root nodes as its starting and ending points and the relationship among sibling nodes. Instead of using fixed sequence layers, we create a GT for each data and train GTNN according to the tree’s structure. GTNNs are data-driven learning in which the number of convolutions varies according to the depth of the tree. Moreover, these models can simultaneously learn various types of datasets through the recursive learning method. Depth-first convolution (DFC) encodes the interaction result from leaf nodes to the root node in a bottom-up approach, and depth-first deconvolution (DFD) decodes the interaction result from the root node to the leaf nodes in a top-down approach. To demonstrate the performance of these networks, we conducted three experiments. In the first experiment, we verified whether it can be processed by combining graph tree neural networks and feature extraction networks(combining association models and feature extraction models). The second experiment is about whether the output of GTNN can embed information on all data contained in the GT(association). In the third experiment, we simultaneously learn the structure of images, sounds, graphs, and trees structure datasets (processing various datasets). We compared the performance of existing networks that separately learned image, sound, and natural language, relation datasets with the performance simultaneously learned by connecting these networks. As a result, these models learned without significant performance degradation, and the output vector contained all the information in the GT. And the importance of transfer-learning and fine-tuning in these models is explained.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

In deep learning, various architectures have been designed such as CNN, RNN, and GNN(LeCun et al., 1989; Hopfield, 1982; Scarselli et al., 2008; Wu et al., 2020). These networks show good performance in various fields such as image, text, and sound etc. However, most of the existing studies are focused on a specific dataset or task. And there are many tasks that humans can do but are difficult to perform with neural networks.

First, some of the difficulties with imitating human neural networks are introduced below. (C-i) Sensory organs that process information for each data type exist at several starting points. (C-ii) A tree cannot express the relationship between sibling nodes, and it is difficult to define the graph’s starting point and ending point. (C-iii) The structure that receives information is hierarchical and freely integrated with various structures. But existing networks are designed to learn only specific tasks or datasets because layers are fixed. (C-iv) The number of activated neurons and the processing depths differ depending on the data type and complexity. And sometimes the information is entered and sometimes not. (C-v) Human neurons are very numerous and process various types of data.

C-[ _·_ ] denotes the characteristics. (C-i) Humans have various sensory organs from which they receive information such as sight, hearing, and smell, and transfer it to the cerebral cortex using different

lobe, and the auditory nerve begins with the auditory cortex of the temporal lobe; this means that the two paths have different starting points. (C-ii) A graph can describe the relationship depending on whether all nodes are connected, but it is not easy to define the start and end points. A tree is a data structure that can define direction with the leaf and root nodes. On the other hand, a tree structure cannot express the relationship between sibling nodes. (C-iii) Information from different sensory organs is gathered in the association area. Typically, the posterior parietal cortex(Malach et al., 1995) is located at the top of the head as part of the parietal lobe, and sensory information such as vision and hearing is fused and interpreted; Then, information is coordinated in the frontal lobe and humans act. (C-iv) There is also an over-fitting problem in DNN(Widrow et al., 1960), and various attempts have been made to solve this in (Srivastava et al., 2014). In DNN, simply deeper layers lead to an over-fitting problem(Dai et al., 2017). In particular, NasNet(Zoph et al., 2018) has attempted to design a layer automatically through reinforcement learning and RNN. This means that there is an appropriate network depth depending on the complexity of the dataset; we want to design a network that could adjust the depth of the layers according to the complexity of each data, not the data set. (C-v) Also, the number of human neurons is about 85 billion(Herculano-Houzel, 2009), which is difficult to express in a network. Therefore, GTNNs are designed to solve these problems.

A-[ _·_ ] denotes our approach of C-[ _·_ ]. (A-i) This network can perform information processing, such as that done by sensory organs before their signals are entered into the cerebral cortex. In this paper, we express this sensory organ as a feature-extraction process(sec.2.2). Type information is stored with the data and converted into an input vector by different feature extraction processes for each type. (A-ii) We propose a new data structure called Graph Tree Node(GTN) and Graph Tree(GT). A graph tree can be expressed using leaf and root nodes as the starting and ending points, as well as the relationship among sibling nodes. In addition, we can modify the existing tree dataset at no high cost. (A-iii) subtrees of different types are merged and make a final decision at the root node; this structure is difficult to express with the existing forward-learning method. Therefore, we used a recursive-learning method(Goller & Kuchler, 1996). This methodology has been effective in analyzing the semantics of programming source code or natural language(Socher et al., 2013; Mou et al., 2014). We call the recursive-convolution methodology used in GTNNs the depth-first convolution(DFC) methodology(sec.2.3). DFC is a convolution method in which subtrees originating from different Leaf nodes are integrated into a bottom-up approach and represent hierarchical and relational information. GTNNs can simultaneously learn various types of datasets through the recursive learning method because the graph tree data structure can express various model structures. (A-iv) GTNNs are data-driven learning in which the number of convolutions varies according to the depth of the tree. Instead of using fixed sequence layers, we create a graph tree for each data and learn according to the tree’s structure. If we modify this network further, we can adjust the depth according to the type and complexity of each data. In addition, We introduce two models of GTNNs called graph tree convolutional networks(GTC) and graph tree recursive networks(GTR), and these models are mathematically related to MLP(Hinton et al., 2006) and RNN. The Fully Connected layer(FC layer) in MLP as Level Layer of GTC, and Time in RNN as depth of GTR on a special case. (A-v) The number of human neurons is enormous, and it is difficult to express all of their structures in a network; on the other hand, this network can represent multiple neurons because it can perform recursive end-to-end learning according to the amount of information.

This network theory holds that "units of information have a relationship in the form of a graph, then become a bigger unit of information, and have a relationship with other units of information. At this point, the unit of information is a set of neurons, and we can express it as a vector with GTNN."

To demonstrate the performance of this network, we conducted three experiments. And we used several benchmark datasets (image(MNIST(LeCun et al., 1998)), sound(Speech Commands(Warden, 2018)), text(IMDB(Maas et al., 2011),SST(Socher et al., 2013)), graph(Dou et al., 2021)).

In the first experiment, It verifies whether feature extraction networks and association networks can be learned together. we compared the performance of existing networks that separately learned image(LeNet-5(LeCun et al., 1989), sound(M5(Dai et al., 2017)), text(CNN(Kim, 2014)) with the performance simultaneously learned by connecting these networks for feature extraction into GTNN.

The second experiment contained one or three types of data into a GT, and GTNNs learned these GTs. Then, we verified whether the output contained all the information inside the GT. Then, we conducted a verification experiment on whether the network’s output contained all information on the tree.

In the third experiment, we verifies whether data of various structures(image, sound, tree, graph) can be learned and checked the plot of the learning process in appendix E. As a result, the network was learned without significant degradation in performance compared to when learning existing networks separately, and all information on the tree could be embedded as a vector.

## 2 GRAPH TREE NEURAL NETWORKS

In this section, we proposes graph tree architecture. The architecture consists of three parts
(i) Defining the data structure of graph tree node(GTN) and designing the graph tree structure (GT).

(ii) Defining the feature-extraction(FE) process. (iii) Defining the GTNN model.

## 2.1 GRAPH TREE DATA STRUCTURE

The graph tree (GT) is a data structure that can express relational and hierarchical information.

- _x_ : (Input) – we can store the data such as images, sound, text, or tabular to each node.

- _τ_ : (Type) – This refers to the type of information matching the input _x_ . the feature-extraction function( _ψ_ ) is selected by _τ_ like Eqn.1.
- **A** _c_ : (Children Adjacency Matrix) – This refers to the relationship information that exists in GTN. the number of children is _N_ and we can express it as **A** _c ∈_ R _[N][×][N]_ .

- **C** : (Children) – This refers to the child nodes of GTN matching to the node of **A** _c_ . If there is no matching child, it is replaced with **GTN** _∅_ , which carries the initial hidden state _[−→]_ 0 instead. We can express this as **C** = _{_ **GTN** 1 _,_ **GTN** 2 _, .._ **GTN** _N }_ .

We can express **GTN** = _{x, τ,_ **A** _c,_ **C** _}_ , **GTN** _i ∈_ **GT** . **GTN** _root_ denotes the root node of **GT** . The reason for defining the relationship among child nodes is the convenience of implementation. If we define GTN in the above way, we can convert the tree dataset into the GT dataset without significantly modifying the tree structure that has previously been useful.

## 2.2 FEATURE EXTRACTION NETWORKS & TYPE BIAS

The _x_ (input) and _τ_ (type) exist together in GTN, and the feature extraction process of _x_ is selected by _τ_ as Eqn 1. We expressed the function of feature extraction as Ψ, and this function converts _x_ to _[−→] x_ . And the one-hot vector has the effect of having a different bias value for each type( _τ_ ). The weight parameter corresponding to the type one-hot vector means the bias value for the corresponding type, and the activation threshold value is adjusted for each type-bias. It can represent data type information and that it is transmitted from which nerve. In addition, we do not need to perform adding operations(+) for bias. Therefore, the empty space of GT can be used as a zero-vector when batch learning is performed. And the dictionary structure is very useful for batch learning; we attached the methodology to appendix.A. This methodology divides mini-batch samples by type, and the type-mini-batch size varies each time. Therefore, we recommend using a weight standardization(Qiao et al., 2019) or group normalization(Wu & He, 2018) to avoid batch normalization(Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015) affected by the batch size. We described the details of the network we used in appendix.C.

## 2.4 ASSOCIATION NEURAL NETWORK MODELS : GTR & GTC SERIES

This section proposes GTR and GTC series that perform inductive learning in GTNNs. We describe the convolution performed in one graph tree node by DFC. These networks can be used as an association cell or layer, and we explained the difference between the two models in appendix B.

These association models do not use any fixed architecture and integrate information according to the GT structure. And by fusing graph and tree structures, we can learn more diverse data structures with one cell and various architectures can be expressed by data(please see the appendix.D). Accordingly, these models are freer to perform various tasks and learn a lot of information using fewer parameters.

These association models are as follows: The feature extraction models create the information from data; It is the "smallest unit" of information in the GT. The parent GTN receives information from their children and perform the convolution operation with the relation term; the convolution output is a "bigger unit" of information. Finally, a root GTN has all GT information and perform the convolution operation; the output is "the biggest unit" of information.

## 3 EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS

This section introduces the contents of the experiment and the GT dataset consisting of the image, sound, and natural language. How to design GT is introduced in appendix.D.

**Datasets** Our goal is to express the information delivery process of existing models as a graph tree of data. The first experiment is whether various datasets can be simultaneously learned by combining GTNN and feature extraction networks. the GT we used in the experiment is illustrated by Fig.4(a). Layer node means that it has no input value, only performs convolution. If there is no sibling node, GTC performs the same operation as one FC layer. Sound and natural language GT have two nodes, and image GT has three nodes because It was configured similarly to figure (likes this fig.1). The

The second experiment is about whether the output( _[−→] h root_ ) of GTNN is embedded information on all data contained in the GT(association). Therefore, we performed image, sound, and text classification by putting image, sound, text data in one GT, and we also learned the GT used in the first experiment simultaneously. One problem is that when the three types of data are combined, The number of combinations of the dataset becomes too huge. For example, there are 50,000 images, 105,000 sounds, and 17,500 natural language samples, and when these data are combined, we need to generate 50,000 _×_ 105,000 _×_ 17,500 GT samples. For this problem, we used a sampling method. When loading each data, two data of different types are sampled randomly. Thus, when we load one data, two GTs are generated of the one type GT and the three type GT. progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper introduces a novel architectural paradigm—Graph Tree Neural Networks (GTNNs)—positioned as a unifying framework addressing limitations in conventional deep learning models, such as rigid layer structures, inability to model complex inter-node relationships, and constraints in multi-modal or cross-domain learning. While the motivation is ambitious and conceptually intriguing—drawing biological inspiration from human neural processing and attempting to unify diverse data modalities (images, sound, graphs, trees) within a single, adaptable architecture—the presentation suffers from significant issues that undermine its credibility and scientific rigor. The writing is often vague, lacks precise technical formalization, and fails to clearly distinguish the proposed Graph Tree (GT) structure from existing hierarchical or message-passing architectures in graph neural networks or tree-based models like recursive neural networks. Key concepts such as “depth-first convolution” and “depth-first deconvolution” are introduced informally without rigorous mathematical definitions or comparisons to established notions of message passing, making it difficult to assess novelty or correctness. Furthermore, the claim of "simultaneously learning various types of datasets" without performance degradation is extraordinary, yet the experiments are inadequately described, lack baselines, implementation details, or statistical validation, offering no reproducible evidence for such strong claims. The abstraction level fluctuates unpredictably, mixing high-level neurobiological analogies with underspecified network mechanics, which harms clarity. Most critically, the paper lacks a coherent theoretical or empirical thread that convincingly demonstrates how GTNNs outperform—or even differ meaningfully from—existing models on well-established benchmarks. As a result, despite an imaginative premise and potential relevance to multi-modal representation learning, the paper’s contributions remain unsubstantiated and poorly grounded in the standards expected by the ICLR community. Substantial revision, formalization, and empirical validation would be required to support its central claims.

comments.title: Overstated claim about output containing all information in GT
comments.quote: As a result, these models learned without significant performance degradation, and the output vector contained all the information in the GT.
comments.explanation: The claim that the output vector 'contains all the information in the GT' is too strong and unsupported. This phrasing implies perfect, lossless reconstruction—equivalent to injectivity or invertibility—which is not justified by experiments alone. While downstream task performance or reconstruction fidelity may support preservation of *sufficient* or *task-relevant* information, it does not entail retention of *all* structural and node-level details. The paper lacks theoretical proof, probe experiments, or perfect reconstruction metrics to back this claim. A more accurate phrasing would be that the model preserves high-fidelity or reconstructable information. This issue appears in multiple forms (e.g., in abstract and Section 1) and represents a conceptual overreach with implications for how the model's representational capacity is interpreted.

comments.title: Incorrect normalization in GCN-style adjacency transformation
comments.quote: Therefore, **D**[˜] _cp_ 2 **[A]**[˜] _cp_ **[D]**[˜] _cp_ 2 is applied using the order matrix **D**[˜] _cp_ of **A**[˜] _cp_ as a method of normalizing the relationship matrix in Eqn. 3.
comments.explanation: The expression **D̃**_cp^(1/2) **Ã**_cp **D̃**_cp^(1/2) is incorrectly stated and contradicts both standard GCN normalization (Kipf & Welling, 2016) and the paper’s own intent. The correct form should be **D̃**_cp^−1/2 **Ã**_cp **D̃**_cp^−1/2 to perform symmetric normalization that prevents scale imbalances from node degrees. The quoted form would upscale connected nodes—opposite to normalization. Although OCR may have corrupted '−1/2' into '2', the text describes normalization, so the formula contradicts both logic and standard practice. This undermines the mathematical foundation of the GTR aggregation mechanism and risks incorrect implementation.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in GTAs attention mechanism definition
comments.quote: GTAs (Graph Tree Attention Networks) extend GTC with per-level attention: **GTAs** = {{**W**_0, ..., **W**_m}, {[→]a_0, ..., [→]a_m}, Ψ, g, σ, σ_a}, where [→]a_lv ∈ ℝ^[2F_lv]^ and σ_a is LeakyReLU.
comments.explanation: There is a critical dimension mismatch in the GTAs model: the attention parameter [→]a_lv is defined in ℝ^[2F_lv]^, but the attention computation (Eqn. e10) operates on the transformed embeddings **W**[→]h′, which are of dimension F′_lv. The concatenated vector before attention is therefore size 2F′_lv, not 2F_lv. Unless F_lv = F′_lv (which the paper does not assume, as GTAs support variable dimensions per level), the dot product [→]a_lv^T(⋅) is undefined. This suggests a fundamental flaw in the model specification—either the attention should be applied pre-transformation (on raw inputs) or the parameter should be defined over 2F′_lv. This breaks the claimed design and impedes reproducibility.

comments.title: Underspecified aggregation function and its role in DFD and GTR
comments.quote: g denotes the aggregate function, and σ denotes the activation function.
comments.explanation: The aggregation function g is central to both the forward (DFC) and backward (DFD) passes but is never formally defined. While the paper mentions 'g was the readout of Max', it provides no mathematical expression (e.g., max-pooling over child states) or justification for this choice. The invertibility of DFD depends on g: max-pooling can be reversed with indices, but sum or mean cannot. The model uses stored indices (∗i_2), suggesting g = max, but this is not stated. Furthermore, it is unclear whether g is applied to hidden states, input-hidden pairs, or attention-weighted sums. The lack of specification affects reproducibility and undermines connections to standard models (e.g., GCNs use sum aggregation). The omission is especially serious given claims of invertibility and mathematical equivalence to RNNs.

comments.title: Missing justification for root node's feature not being convolved with children's outputs
comments.quote: The important thing is that the root node only receives information from their children and does not the convolution with the root feature vector.
comments.explanation: The root node has an input feature _x_root_ and type _τ_root_, yet the text claims it does not perform convolution with its own feature vector during aggregation. This deviates from standard recursive models (e.g., Tree-LSTM), which combine local input with child states. While Algorithm 3 applies **GTNN** to [_x’_root, _h’_root], suggesting the feature is used in a final transformation, the lack of explanation for why the root is not processed like internal nodes is a conceptual gap. Is this to prioritize relational over local information? To isolate global readout? Without justification, the design appears arbitrary and weakens the architectural coherence.

comments.title: Contradictions and ambiguities in Algorithm 1 structure and variable handling
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 contains multiple OCR-induced or formatting errors that impair reproducibility: (1) The for-loop line is garbled, with '6:5:' and malformed syntax suggesting 'for q ← pq' instead of 'for q from 1 to N_p'. (2) Output of Ψ includes ∗i_1, which contradicts its deterministic definition in Equation (1) and should be generated by aggregation, not embedding. (3) Leaf node handling assigns an incomplete expression to GTN_p.more, leaving ∗i_2 undefined, which breaks DFD. (4) Input to **GTNN** appears to include **x**_p' and **h**_p' before they are computed. (5) Superscripts like '[′]' are misplaced or duplicated. Together, these create confusion about state flow, stacking order, and auxiliary output generation. While context resolves some, the algorithm as presented is not executable.

comments.title: Underspecified children adjacency matrix initialization and learnability
comments.quote: the number of children is _N_ and we can express it as **A** _c ∈_ R _[N][×][N]_ .
comments.explanation: The paper defines **A**_c ∈ ℝ^{N×N} but does not specify whether it is binary or real-valued, fixed or learnable. If it represents sibling relationships, how are these inferred? Is **A**_c hand-crafted, learned from data, or initialized as identity? Algorithm 1 treats it as input, suggesting it's static, but the text claims it 'allows expressing relationships among siblings'—implying it carries learned structure. Without clarification on how **A**_c is derived or updated, the model's ability to capture inter-sibling dependencies is underspecified. This is especially critical as **A**_c is used in GCN-style normalization, where weight scales affect training.

comments.title: Asymmetry in DFC and DFD traversal ordering lacks justification
comments.quote: If DFC proceeds from left to right as left first (1 ...N ), DFD proceeds from right to left as right first ( N... 1). Please see Fig.3.
comments.explanation: The paper mandates left-first traversal for DFC and right-first for DFD, implying intentional reversal. However, no rationale is provided. In autoencoder architectures, symmetric traversal order is typical unless the model is designed for permutation-invariant processing or reversible computation. The reversal may introduce misalignment during reconstruction, especially if **A**_c is not permutation-invariant. Unless sibling attention or **A**_c learning compensates, this asymmetry risks instability or information loss. The figure may illustrate the intent, but a textual or theoretical justification is missing.

comments.title: Inconsistent treatment of type bias as embedding versus additive bias
comments.explanation: The paper claims the one-hot type vector in the input acts as a 'bias value' that 'adjusts the activation threshold'. This is conceptually incorrect: a concatenated one-hot embedding functions as a learnable input modulator (like FiLM), not an additive bias. The mechanism allows different weights per type but does not directly shift the activation threshold like a bias term. The statement 'we do not need to perform adding operations(+) for bias' is misleading, as bias terms can still be beneficial. While the method may work, this mischaracterization could confuse readers about the role and design of bias in neural models.

comments.title: Underspecified attention mechanism in GTRAs
comments.quote: We introduce GTRAs that learn the importance through attention by slightly modifying the expression of the GTR.
comments.explanation: The description of GTRAs lacks sufficient algorithmic detail. While it references GATs and mentions LeakyReLU and attention parameters, it does not specify how attention scores are computed (e.g., no formula for e_{qr}). The statement 'replaced with the part to which the adjacency matrix is connected' is unclear: does attention substitute or modulate **A**_c? Without access to Eqn.7, readers cannot determine how child states are weighted during aggregation. Multi-head setup is described, but attention computation remains a black box. This prevents reproduction of the GTRAs variant.

comments.title: Questionable claim of 100.0% accuracy on IMDB sentiment task
comments.explanation: The paper reports 100.0% test accuracy on the IMDB sentiment dataset, which is implausible given the known label noise and subjective nature of the task. State-of-the-art models typically achieve 95–97%. Such a perfect score suggests data leakage, overfitting, or an unreported data-cleaning step. The authors do not provide error analysis, dataset modifications, or justification for avoiding overfitting despite using deep models. This result, if not carefully qualified, risks misleading readers about the model’s generalization capacity.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Definition of Graph Tree vs. Standard Tree/Graph Structures
comments.quote: A graph tree(GT) can express leaf and root nodes as its starting and ending points and the relationship among sibling nodes.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that the authors were conflating standard graph and tree data structures. Trees already have root and leaf nodes, and graphs can encode sibling relationships via edges, so this claim seemed redundant. However, upon reviewing Section 2.1, it appears the authors define a Graph Tree Node (GTN) with a Children Adjacency Matrix (**A**_c), which explicitly models relationships among children—something standard trees do not. This design choice allows modeling of inter-sibling dependencies (e.g., attention or lateral connections), which standard recursive models (like Tree-RNNs) lack. The intent is clear in context: GT generalizes trees by adding sibling relations. However, the term 'graph tree' is non-standard and could mislead readers into thinking it’s a hybrid of arbitrary graphs and trees. The authors could clarify that GT is a rooted tree with an auxiliary graph structure among siblings, not an arbitrary DAG or cyclic graph. Since this is resolved with context, the issue is more presentational than technical.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of Notation in Children Adjacency Matrix
comments.quote: **A** _c_ : (Children Adjacency Matrix) – ... we can express it as **A** _c ∈_ R _[N][×][N]_ .
comments.explanation: At first glance, the notation R_[N][×][N] appears to be an OCR error for ℝ^{N×N}, which is standard for real-valued matrices. Given that the text elsewhere uses standard math notation (e.g., R _[F][ ′][×]_[(] _[F]_[ +] _[B]_[+] _[F][ ′]_[)]), this is likely a formatting issue during PDF conversion. The intended meaning—**A**_c ∈ ℝ^{N×N}—is clear from context: it's an adjacency matrix among N children. Since this would not affect a reader's understanding and aligns with the definition of modeling sibling relationships, no actual error remains. The symbol is consistent in usage wherever **A**_c appears later.

comments.title: Unjustified Claim About Output Containing All Information in GT
comments.quote: As a result, these models learned without significant performance degradation, and the output vector contained all the information in the GT.
comments.explanation: This is a strong information-preserving claim. At first, I thought this might be a theoretical assertion backed by reconstruction or injectivity proofs. However, the claim is based on experimental verification (second experiment), not formal proof. While the experiment may test whether information can be reconstructed from the output (e.g., via decoding), 'containing all information' is a very high bar—equivalent to lossless compression or injective representation. In practice, due to finite capacity and continuous embeddings, exact information retention is impossible without explicit design (e.g., invertible networks). The authors likely mean 'sufficient' or 'task-relevant' information is preserved, as shown by downstream decoding. But phrasing it as 'all the information' overreaches. Context slightly mitigates this: Appendix E (not shown) may contain details, and the propagate function in Algo.3 suggests an autoencoder setup. Still, without evidence of perfect reconstruction, this claim is too strong. A more accurate phrasing would be 'the output can reconstruct input data with high fidelity'.

comments.title: Missing Justification for DFC Not Using Root's Feature Vector
comments.quote: The important thing is that the root node only receives information from their children and does not the convolution with the root feature vector.
comments.explanation: This raised concern because the root node has an input _x_ and type _τ_, so it should contribute features like any other node. Not using the root’s own feature vector during final convolution (unless handled separately) may cause information loss. The authors say convolution is 'performed once more on the output of the DFC' (referring to Algo.3), but it’s unclear how the root’s _x_ is incorporated. In Algo.1, DFC processes _x_p via Ψ first, but in line 15, the parent uses only child-aggregated info. In Algo.3, **GTNN** applies to [_x’_root, _h’_root]—suggesting _x’_root is used there. So perhaps the root’s feature is used in the final layer, not in-tree. This seems like a design choice: features are only propagated upward, and root processing happens post-DFC. While this is implementable, the lack of explanation for why the root node’s feature isn't convolved with its children's output like internal nodes is a gap. Standard recursive models (e.g., Tree-LSTM) do combine child states and local input. This deviation needs justification—e.g., to prioritize relational over local information, or to mimic cortical integration. Without it, the design seems arbitrary.

comments.title: Underspecified Aggregate Function and Its Role in DFD
comments.quote: g denotes the aggregate function, and σ denotes the activation function.
comments.explanation: The aggregation function _g_ is central to how information is combined from children, yet it is never defined—neither in the text nor in an appendix referenced in the context. While common choices exist (sum, mean, max), the DFD algorithm uses stored indices (∗i_2) possibly for unpooling, suggesting _g_ may be max-pooling. However, this is not stated. If _g_ is summation, DFD cannot exactly reverse it without additional normalization. The model’s invertibility (implied by autoencoder use) depends heavily on _g_. For example, max-pooling is partially invertible with indices, but sum-pooling is not. The lack of specification makes reproduction impossible. Appendix D (cited) may define it, but even so, the main text should clarify whether _g_ is learnable or fixed, and how it is inverted during DFD. This is a methodological gap.

comments.title: Confusion Between 'Recursive-Learning Method' and Standard Recursion
comments.quote: Therefore, we used a recursive-learning method(Goller & Kuchler, 1996). This methodology has been effective in analyzing the semantics of programming source code or natural language(Socher et al., 2013; Mou et al., 2014).
comments.explanation: At first, I interpreted 'recursive-learning method' as a reference to recursive neural networks (Tree-RNNs), which is correct. However, the phrase 'recursive-convolution methodology' used later to describe DFC may mislead readers into thinking it is a new learning paradigm. In fact, DFC is a recursive algorithm (in the computer science sense) applied to traverse and process a tree, not necessarily a fundamentally new learning method. The authors correctly cite Socher et al. (2013) for recursive neural models, so the intent is clear: they build on recursive processing. However, the framing suggests novelty where the core idea—bottom-up tree traversal with learned transformations—is well-established. The innovation lies in the sibling adjacency matrix and the data-driven structure, not the recursion mechanism. This is more a matter of emphasis than error, but could mislead readers about the paper’s technical contribution.

comments.title: Ambiguous Role of Root Node in Final Convolution Step
comments.quote: The important thing is that the root node only receives information from their children and does not the convolution with the root feature vector. Therefore, like algo.3, convolution is performed once more on the output of the DFC.
comments.explanation: At first, I interpreted this statement as suggesting that the root node does not use its own input _x_root_ during the DFC process — only aggregating from children. This would align with a standard message-passing setup where the root's own feature is ignored unless explicitly incorporated. However, the text claims the DFC output is further processed 'once more' via **GTNN**, which appears in Algorithm 3: _h_root ←_ **GTNN**(I, _x′_root, _h′_root, 0). This raises concerns: (1) What is the input I? Unexplained. (2) Why apply a separate **GTNN** operation post-DFC? If DFC already produces _[−→]_x'_root_, _[−→]_h'_root_, is this a final aggregation or a transformation layer? The model definition in Sec 2.4.1 does not mention this extra step. In Algorithm 1, the root node is processed like any other internal node (lines 4–7), so it already performs a convolution using its own _x_p_ and child states. Thus, applying **GTNN** again seems redundant or possibly double-counting the root's features. The authors may intend this final **GTNN** call as a root-level readout or global representation refinement. But without a formal definition of **GTNN** in this context, and with no matching operation in Algorithm 1, this creates ambiguity. The discrepancy between Algorithm 1 and Algorithm 3 undermines reproducibility. A careful reader could conclude that either the root node is improperly handled or the final propagation duplicates work.

comments.title: Inconsistent Treatment of Type Bias in Equation (1) vs. Text
comments.explanation: Equation (1) concatenates the transformed input _ψτ(x)_ with a one-hot vector of the type _τ_, implying that type information is directly embedded in the input vector. The text explains this as providing a 'different bias value for each type' and says the 'weight parameter corresponding to the type one-hot vector means the bias value'. However, this reasoning conflates embedding with biasing. A one-hot _τ_ in the input vector acts as a learnable embedding that modulates weights, not as a bias in the neural sense (additive offset). The claim that this 'adjusts the activation threshold' is misleading: bias terms do that; input embeddings scale or shift response curves depending on weights. Moreover, saying 'we do not need to perform adding operations(+) for bias' confuses architecture design: biases can still be necessary even with type embeddings. The model may work well in practice, but the justification is conceptually inaccurate. Standard practice (e.g., FiLM, Adapters) separates modulation from bias. While this doesn't break the method, it risks misleading readers about the mechanism.

comments.title: Underspecified Children Adjacency Matrix Domain
comments.quote: the number of children is _N_ and we can express it as **A** _c ∈_ R _[N][×][N]_ .
comments.explanation: The matrix **A**_c is defined as being in R^[N]×[N], suggesting real-valued entries, but the surrounding context implies a binary adjacency matrix indicating sibling relationships. In Sec 2.1, it's described as 'relationship information that exists in GTN', and in Sec 2.4.1, the GCN-style normalization (**D̃**^(-1/2)**Ã**_cp**D̃**^(-1/2)) is applied, which typically assumes unweighted or weighted edges. But if **A**_c is real-valued, where do the weights come from? The text never specifies whether **A**_c is binary or learnable, nor how it is initialized or updated. In Algorithm 1, **A**_c is passed into **GTNN** as a fixed input, suggesting it's static. But if it's static, how are sibling relationships learned? If it's learnable, the update rule is missing. The claim that 'this allows expressing relationships among siblings' is valid only if **A**_c is meaningful. Without clarification, a reader cannot reproduce the model: Is **A**_c hand-crafted? Is it learned? Is it identity for no relations? This is a real gap in method specification.

comments.title: Asymmetry in DFC and DFD Ordering Not Fully Justified
comments.quote: If DFC proceeds from left to right as left first (1 ...N ), DFD proceeds from right to left as right first ( N... 1). Please see Fig.3.
comments.explanation: The text mandates that DFC uses left-first and DFD uses right-first traversal, implying a deliberate reversal. At first, I assumed this might be for symmetry or reversible computation, as in invertible networks. But no justification is given. In autoencoders, symmetric traversal (e.g., left-first in both) is common unless there's a structural reason for reversal. The paper mentions implementing DFD as pre-order while DFC is post-order — which alone creates asymmetry. But reversing the child order (right-first) adds an extra asymmetry. Does this help reconstruction? Prevent interference? The figure (not visible) may illustrate it, but without visual or formal explanation, a reader might suspect a bug or unnecessary complexity. Standard DFS traversal for tree autoencoders (e.g., in TreeRNNs) preserves child order. Unless the **A**_c matrix encodes order-sensitive relations, this reversal risks misalignment. The issue is resolvable only if **A**_c is permutation-invariant, but that's not stated. Given that **GTNN** uses **A**_c in aggregation, the order might matter. This underspecification could affect training stability.

comments.title: Unjustified Claim of 'All Information Embedded' in Output
comments.quote: As a result, the network was learned without significant degradation in performance compared to when learning existing networks separately, and all information on the tree could be embedded as a vector.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the output vector 'contains all information in the GT' — a very strong statement. This appears in both the abstract and Section 1. But what does 'all information' mean? Full reconstructibility? Sufficient statistics for all nodes? The experiments only verify performance on downstream tasks and possibly reconstruction (implied by Algorithm 3), but there's no evidence of *complete* information preservation. For example, can any node’s input be recovered from _h_root_? The paper lacks probe experiments, ablation on information flow, or theoretical proof of injectivity. In deep learning, even perfect accuracy doesn’t imply full embedding of input data — redundancy and compression are expected. While the DFD process suggests reconstruction is possible, its success is not quantitatively shown in the passage. The claim overreaches what is demonstrated; 'sufficient for task performance' would be accurate, but 'all information' is not supported.

comments.title: Inconsistent variable usage in DFC algorithm and description
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned that the output of Ψ in line 3 is written as (_x p, ∗i_1_), but the text and surrounding notation suggest that Ψ maps (x, τ) to a feature vector [−→]x_p = [ψ_τ(x), onehot(τ)]. The inclusion of ∗i_1 as an output from Ψ is unexpected, since earlier in Section 2.2, Ψ is defined deterministically via Equation (1) without auxiliary outputs such as indices. This seems to conflict with the paper’s definition. However, upon closer inspection, ∗i_1 appears later in DFD (Algorithm 2) as index information saved in GTN.more (e.g., for max-pooling unpooling), which implies that Ψ might be overloaded to return both the embedded vector and some preprocessing metadata. But this contradicts the original definition of Ψ, which does not involve indexing. The context does not resolve this: if Ψ is simply the feature extractor, then it should not return ∗i_1. A more plausible explanation is that this is an OCR issue or a confusion between Ψ and the downstream GTNN operation. However, since the variable ∗i_1 appears in a structural role (saving indices for deconvolution), and Ψ is not described as performing such indexing, the responsibility for generating ∗i_1 likely lies with the GTNN aggregation (e.g., g = max), not Ψ. Therefore, assigning it to the output of Ψ in line 3 is misleading or erroneous. This affects the reproducibility of the algorithm.

comments.title: Ambiguity in stacking and aggregation input in GTR description
comments.explanation: The notation ∥[N] q=0[p] is non-standard and ambiguous—possibly intended to denote vertical concatenation (stacking) over child indices q from 1 to Np. This appears to match the intent of forming a matrix from child feature-hidden pairs. However, in line 14–15 of Algorithm 1, the **GTNN** operation receives a tangled expression: ( _ChildrenList_ **A**_c,_ **x**_′p[, **h**_′][p][, lv] ). The syntax is malformed. It seems the authors mean that GTNN takes the stacked child outputs (from ChildrenList), the adjacency matrix **A**_c, possibly the parent's own feature **x**_p', and the current level lv. But **x**_p' has not yet been computed at this point (it was only embedded earlier as [−→]x_p). Moreover, the parent's own feature should not be included in the aggregation over children. This contradicts the stated bottom-up flow: the parent node should aggregate children first, then possibly combine with its own feature. The equation also shows the output as [−→h_p], but the return is [_−→]x_p, _[−→]h_p], suggesting the parent’s embedded input is preserved and passed up. However, in line 15, the variables are bound as _[−→]x_p' and _[−→]h_p', but only h_p is used in later steps—this introduces confusion about whether the parent’s feature is reused. Given that Section 2.4.1 states the parent performs convolution on child information, not its own, this mixing of parent and child inputs at the aggregation stage is underspecified and potentially incorrect.

comments.title: Misalignment between text description and Algorithm 1 in leaf node handling
comments.explanation: The text states that in a leaf node, the feature vector [−→]x_p and initial hidden state [−→]_0 are returned—this matches line 12. However, line 11 assigns ( _∗i_ 1) _−→_ to GTN_p.more, which is incomplete and syntactically malformed. It appears to be an OCR artifact (perhaps meant to be (∗i_1) ← something), but in context, leaf nodes should also potentially store auxiliary information (e.g., for deconvolution). However, no index ∗i_1 is generated by Ψ or elsewhere in a leaf node unless the feature extraction process itself produces indices (which it does not per Eqn.1). The more serious issue is that the code fragment is garbled: ( _∗i_ 1) _−→_ is not valid assignment syntax. Even accounting for OCR, it’s unclear what is being stored in GTN.more at a leaf. This matters because the DFD algorithm later assumes GTN.more contains both ∗i_1 and ∗i_2. If leaf nodes only set ∗i_1 (as line 11 suggests), then ∗i_2 remains undefined. Moreover, Algorithm 2 line 3 expects both ∗i_1 and ∗i_2. This creates a state mismatch: non-leaf nodes store both in line 16, but leaf nodes only seem to store one (and incorrectly). This is a real gap in the algorithm specification.

comments.title: Incorrect or garbled syntax in Algorithm 1 for-loop line
comments.explanation: The line is clearly corrupted, likely due to OCR or formatting loss. The label '6:5:' suggests a merge of line 5 and 6. The for-loop syntax is nonsensical: '_q−→x[′] ←pq,_ 1 _[−→] h...N[′] pq p←_' appears to be a mangled version of something like 'for q ← 1 to Np do'. The comment '▷ left-first' supports this. The authors likely meant a standard loop over child indices. While this can be reconstructed from context (recursive calls over C_p[q]), the presented pseudocode is not executable or clearly interpretable. This affects reproducibility, especially for readers trying to implement the algorithm directly from the paper. Given that the surrounding description mentions 'left-first' traversal and recursion, the intent is clear—but the presentation falls short. This would normally be a minor formatting issue, but since it's in a core algorithm and the syntax is actively misleading (e.g., suggesting variable assignments inside the loop guard), it crosses into moderate severity.

comments.title: Contradiction in root node processing between text and Algorithm 3
comments.quote: The important thing is that the root node only receives information from their children and does not the convolution with the root feature vector. Therefore, like algo.3, convolution is performed once more on the output of the DFC.
comments.explanation: The text claims the root node does *not* use its own feature vector in convolution, yet in Algorithm 3, after DFC returns [_−→]x'_root, _−→]h'_root], the GTNN function is applied to (I, _−→]x'_root, _−→]h'_root, 0)—suggesting a secondary transformation that includes the encoded root input. This appears to contradict the earlier claim. However, upon deeper reading, the DFC already processes the root's x_p via Ψ (in the general node processing), so _−→]x'_root *does* include the root’s own feature. The 'additional' GTNN step may refer to a global transformation (e.g., readout), which would make sense for an autoencoder bottleneck. The text’s statement likely means that the root does *not perform a recursive aggregation of its own feature*, which is correct—the root aggregates children, then its own feature is encoded and passed up. The clarification in Algorithm 3 suggests that the final vector _−→]h_root is derived from both the child-aggregated state and the root’s feature. So the apparent contradiction is resolved by interpreting the 'convolution with root feature vector' as being part of the standard node processing (which *does* happen), not an additional root-only aggregation. Still, the phrasing 'does not the convolution with the root feature vector' is awkward and misleading. However, since context resolves it, this is a minor ambiguity.

comments.title: Underspecified dimensionality in GTNN aggregation input
comments.explanation: The **GTNN** operation is invoked with what seems to be: ChildrenList (a list of child outputs), **A**_c (adjacency matrix), **x**_′p (possibly the parent’s embedded feature), **h**_′p (child-aggregated hidden state?), and lv. But **x**_′p and **h**_′p are not clearly defined. **h**_′p appears to be the output from DFC—the aggregated child representation—but this is what **GTNN** is supposed to compute. This creates a circular dependency. Moreover, the parent feature **x**_p is computed early (line 3), but it's unclear whether it should be combined before, during, or after child aggregation. In standard recursive neural networks, parent state = f(parent_feature, aggregate(children)). But here, the function signature is confused. The model **GTR** is defined with parameters **W**, Ψ, g, σ—but the aggregation function g is not invoked explicitly in the algorithm. The **GTNN** call is a black box. While some abstraction is acceptable, the omission of how the parent's own feature is fused with the child-aggregated vector makes the method underspecified. Readers cannot reproduce the model without guessing whether it's a sum, concatenation, or gated fusion. The paper later references GCN, suggesting a neighborhood aggregation, but doesn't specify if the self-loop (**A**_c + I) is used during the child-only aggregation or in a broader update.

comments.title: Incorrect normalization in GCN-style adjacency transformation
comments.quote: Therefore, **D**[˜] _cp_ 2 **[A]**[˜] _cp_ **[D]**[˜] _cp_ 2 is applied using the order matrix **D**[˜] _cp_ of **A**[˜] _cp_ as a method of normalizing the relationship matrix in Eqn. 3.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the expression **D**[˜]_cp^(1/2) **A**[˜]_cp **D**[˜]_cp^(1/2) appears to be a misstatement of the standard symmetric normalization used in GCNs (Kipf & Welling, 2016), which is **D**[˜]⁻¹ᐟ² **A**[˜] **D**[˜]⁻¹ᐟ². The quoted text suggests multiplying by **D**[˜]^(1/2) on both sides, which would upscale connected nodes rather than normalize them—opposite to the intended effect. The prose says 'the more connected nodes becames the scale value larger than others' before this, which seems to recognize the problem symmetric normalization solves, yet the proposed fix does the opposite. While OCR might be blamed (e.g., missing superscripts), the surrounding discussion confirms the intent is normalization, so the formula contradicts both standard practice and the authors’ own justification. The correct form should use inverse square roots. This undermines the mathematical foundation of the aggregation step in GTR.

comments.title: Ambiguous indexing in GTR special-case reduction to RNN
comments.quote: If there is no sibling node of the current node, GTR is mathematically identical to the RNN as **IW** [ _[−→] x ,[−→] h_ ]
comments.explanation: The claim that GTR reduces to RNN when no sibling nodes exist is plausible—single-child trees naturally resemble sequences. However, the expression **IW**[[−→]x, [−→]h] is ambiguous. Standard RNNs use **W**[x_t, h_{t−1}] + b, but here **I** is the identity, making **IW** equivalent to **W**, so the notation seems redundant unless **I** serves another role. More critically, the concatenation [x, h] must be weighted by **W**, not simply multiplied by **IW** as written. The phrase suggests a direct matrix multiplication without bias (consistent with type bias via one-hot), but the use of **I** is unmotivated and confusing. It may be an OCR error (perhaps meant to be a block matrix or placeholder), but as written, it distracts from an otherwise valid claim about architectural generalization.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for hidden state dimensions in GTC level layers
comments.explanation: The dimensionality of **W**_lv suggests it maps from (F_lv + B + F'_lv+1) to F'_lv. But earlier definitions state that F is node-feature size, B is type-bias size, and F′ is hidden/output size. The index shift (F'_lv+1) — output size of the child — implies the parent’s weight matrix depends on the child’s output dimension, which is conceptually sound in hierarchical models. However, the notation F'_lv+1 is ambiguous: does it mean F' at level lv+1, or F'_lv incremented by 1? Given that the text says 'the output size of the child is F'_lv+1', and later that F_lv and F'_lv are set to 128 for all levels, this implies F'_lv+1 = 128, same as F'_lv. So the dimension becomes R^[128×(128+3+128)] ≈ R^[128×259], which is well-defined. The notation is awkward but consistent with intent. OCR may have corrupted spacing (e.g., 'F' lv[ ′]_ +1), but context resolves it. Not a real error, just poor formatting.

comments.title: Overstated claim on GTC reducing to MLP when F_lv = 0
comments.quote: If the input size of _Flv_ is 0 and there is no sibling node of the current node, this network is mathematically identical to the FC layer as **IW** _lv[−→] x[T]_ and the MLP as ( **IW** 0 _.._ ( **IW** _lv−_ 1( **IW** _lv[−→] x[T]_ )))
comments.explanation: The claim that GTC reduces to MLP when F_lv = 0 is problematic. If F_lv = 0, the input feature size is zero, meaning no input features—yet the FC layer expression **IW**_lv[−→]x[T] still references [−→]x, which would be empty. This contradicts the definition of Ψ(x,τ) producing [ψ_τ(x), onehot(τ)], where even with zero feature content, the one-hot type bias (B=3) remains. So the actual input dimension is at least B, not F_lv. The reduction only makes sense if 'input size of F_lv' refers to the feature part ψ_τ(x), but the notation conflates F (feature size) with total input size (F+B). Moreover, the expression **IW**_lv[−→]x[T] omits the hidden state from children, which is part of the GTC input stack. The special case requires both single child and feature-free input, but the resulting operation still involves child-to-parent propagation, unlike a standard FC layer. The architectural analogy is suggestive but the mathematical equivalence is not rigorously satisfied.

comments.title: Missing definition of Eqn.3 referenced in normalization step
comments.quote: Therefore, **D**[˜] _cp_ 2 **[A]**[˜] _cp_ **[D]**[˜] _cp_ 2 is applied [...] as a method of normalizing the relationship matrix in Eqn. 3.
comments.explanation: The passage refers to 'Eqn.3' for the mathematical expression of the normalization, but no equation numbers are visible in the provided text. While the full paper likely includes it, within the given context, the absence makes it impossible to verify what 'the mathematical expression of GTC is similar to Eqn 3' means. This is particularly problematic because the normalization process is central to the model’s message-passing mechanism. Readers must infer the form of the aggregation from prose alone. However, since equation references are expected to appear elsewhere in the full paper and the claim about GCN methodology provides sufficient context (A˜ = A + I, degree normalization), this is a forward-reference issue, not a fatal flaw. Still, it affects reproducibility.

comments.title: Underspecified attention mechanism in GTRAs
comments.quote: We introduce GTRAs that learn the importance through attention by slightly modifying the expression of the GTR. [...] With the attention methodology introduced in GATs(Veliˇckovi´c et al., 2017), it learns how the r-th node is of importance to the q-th node. This information is replaced with the part to which the adjacency matrix is connected.
comments.explanation: The description of GTRAs lacks key algorithmic details. It mentions adding parameters {σa, [−→]a} and using LeakyReLU for attention, and references GATs, but does not specify how attention scores are computed—e.g., no version of e_{ij} = a(Wx_i, Wx_j) is given. It later mentions Eqn.7 (not shown), so some formalism may appear there. However, the phrase 'replaced with the part to which the adjacency matrix is connected' is confusing: does attention replace A_cp entries, or modulate them? If A_cp is absent, the model uses sigmoid to learn relationships, but the mechanism isn't described. The multi-head setup is clearer (K=8, head dim=16), but without seeing Eqn.7, the core computation remains underspecified. This prevents reproduction of the attention variant, especially how child states are weighted during aggregation.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of F and F' in GTRAs description
comments.quote: GTRAs (Graph Tree Recursive Attention Networks) extend GTR with attention mechanism: **GTRAs** = {**W**, Ψ, g, σ, σ_a, [→]a}, where σ_a is LeakyReLU and [→]a ∈ ℝ^[2F′]^ is attention parameter vector.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned about the dimension of the attention parameter vector [→]a being in ℝ^[2F′]^, because earlier in the summary, F′ is defined as the output (hidden) size per level. In the multi-head attention setup (point 7), it's stated that K=8 and per-head dim=16 implies F′=128. However, in the attention coefficient equation (e10), e_{qr} = σ_a([→]a^T([**W**[→]h′_q ∥ **W**[→]h′_r])), the concatenated vector is of size 2F′ only if **W** maps to F′, which is consistent. But in the GTRAs definition, no level-specific **W**_lv is mentioned, unlike in GTAs. Given that GTRAs are recursive and presumably level-shared like GTR, and the attention vector [→]a is not indexed by lv, the use of F′ (not F′_lv) may be intentional. While GTAs use [→]a_lv ∈ ℝ^[2F_lv]^ with input size F_lv, GTRAs using F′ suggests they attend over transformed (post-**W**) features. This is a subtle but coherent distinction: GTAs apply attention before transformation (using raw input dim F_lv), while GTRAs apply it after (using hidden dim F′). Thus, the notation is likely consistent with intent. No error found.

comments.title: Ambiguous indexing in Algorithm 1: '∗i 1' and '∗i 2' not formally defined
comments.explanation: The variables '∗i 1' and '∗i 2' appear in Algorithms 1, 2, and 3 without formal definition. Initially, this raised concern about reproducibility, as these appear to be index or intermediate values (e.g., for unpooling). The text mentions storing info during DFC 'like indices for maxpool'. However, despite the lack of explicit definition, the prose in the DFD section clarifies: 'if the aggregate function is a maxpool, we can use the indices information'. So '∗i' likely refers to such indices. The use of '∗i' (with asterisk) suggests placeholders or pointer dereferencing, possibly an OCR artifact from a subscript like 'i_1', but the intent is clear from context. These are auxiliary outputs used in deconvolution and not part of the core model parameters. While underspecified, their role is sufficiently explained in the surrounding text. Fixable by defining them formally.

comments.title: Potential OCR error in Algorithm 1 line 5: ' _q−→x[′] ←pq,_ 1 _[−→] h[′] pq_ '
comments.explanation: This line is garbled due to OCR error. The correct structure should be a loop over children q. The text ' _q−→x[′] ←pq,_ 1 _[−→] h[′] pq_ ' appears to be a misread of 'for q ← 1 to N_p do'. The variable 'q' is used correctly in **C_p[q]**, and the comment '▷ left-first' supports this. Also, children are processed in order. The intended code is clearly: 'for q = 1 to N_p do'. The assignment to _x′_{pq}, h′_{pq}_ happens inside the loop via the recursive DFC call. The OCR corrupted both the loop syntax and the comment alignment. This would confuse a reader trying to parse the algorithm. While the intent is recoverable from context, the syntax is invalid.

comments.title: Inconsistent table formatting affects readability and parsing of results
comments.quote:
|Model|Task layers
MNIST
SC
IMDB|47(=10+35+2) class
MNIST
SC
IMDB|Transfer learning
MNIST
SC
IMDB|Fine tuning
MNIST
SC
